Section 19.7
SALTS OF CARBOXYLIC ACIDS
©2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education .

Neutralization of RCO 2 H
In the presence of strong base (e.g., MOH ), carboxylic acids form carboxylate salts RCO 2 − M +
Metal carboxylates are often soluble in water (they’re ionic salts, after all!)

Micelles
Carboxylates with long alkyl chains have a polar end and a nonpolar end—they are amphiphilic
In a polar solvent, the polar − CO 2 − groups point outward; the hydrophobic alkyl groups cluster inward

Micelles-1
Carboxylates with long alkyl chains have a polar end and a nonpolar end—they are amphiphilic
The resulting spherical structure is called a micelle
In amphiphilic detergents , dirt and grease dissolve in the center of the micelles.

Section 19.8
DICARBOXYLIC ACIDS
Dicarboxylic Acids

−CO 2 H Is Inductively Withdrawing
A nearby − CO 2 H substituent increases the acidity of − CO 2 H via an inductive effect
The effect diminishes as the distance between the groups increases
Note the remarkable acidity of oxalic acid

Section 19.9
CARBONIC ACID

Formation of Carbonic Acid
Carbonic acid contains two hydroxyl groups bound to a common carbonyl group
Carbonic acid is formed via the hydration of CO 2 dissolved in water
Bicarbonate and hydronium form to a small degree: K for the overall process is 4.3 × 10 -7 ( p K 6.4)
Most dissolved CO 2 in water is still CO 2

Ionization Constants of Carbonic Acid
The net equilibrium constant from the last slide is p K a1 for carbonic acid (6.4)
Like all diacids, the second ionization constant p K a2 is much larger (10.2)
Bicarbonate is a weaker acid than carboxylic acids, but is stronger than water and alcohols

Section 19.10
Sources of Carboxylic Acids

Sources of Carboxylic Acids-1
There are many different types of natural carboxylic acids
Fatty acids
Amino acids
Etc.
Industrially, acids are produced via synthetic oxidation
In the lab, nucleophilic attack on CO 2 is also used
Reactions Yielding RCO 2 H
Reactions Yielding RCO 2 H-1
Reactions Yielding RCO 2 H-2

Section 19.11
Synthesis of Carboxylic Acids by the Carboxylation of Grignard Reagents

Nucleophilic Attack on CO 2
Alkyl halides can be converted to carboxylic acids via a three-step sequence involving (a) Grignard reagent formation, (b) nucleophilic attack on CO 2 , and (c) acidification
The central carbon of CO 2 is electrophilic (bound to two withdrawing oxygen atoms)

From RX to RCO 2 H
For this method to work, the alkyl halide cannot contain any acidic groups (no OH or NH)

Section 19.12
SYNTHESIS OF CARBOXYLIC ACIDS BY THE PREPARATION AND HYDROLYSIS OF NITRILES

RCO 2 H via Nitriles
In the last section, the future carbonyl carbon was electrophilic…
…we can also use the nucleophilic carbon in cyanide ( CN − ) as a precursor to a carboxylic acid
Cyanide engages in S N 2 with 1 degree or 2 degrees alkyl halides
Subsequent acidic hydrolysis gives a carboxylic acid
Cyanohydrins undergo hydrolysis under similar conditions to give α - hydroxyacids
OH and NH are well tolerated
Examples

Section 19.13
REACTIONS OF CARBOXYLIC ACIDS: A REVIEW AND A PREVIEW

Reactivity of Carboxylic Acids
Carboxylic acids themselves are acidic
Carboxylate anions can act as nucleophiles at oxygen
The carboxylic acid oxygen can act as a nucleophile
Carboxylic acids can engage in…
Acid halide formation
Esterification
Reduction

Reactions of RCO 2 H
Reaction (section) and comments General equation and specific example
Conversion to acyl chlorides (see Section 13.7) Thionyl chloride reacts with carboxylic acids to yield acyl chlorides.
Lithium aluminum hydride reduction (see Section 16.3) Carboxylic acids are not reducible by catalytic hydrogenation, but are converted to primary alcohols by lithium aluminum hydride. Sodium borohydride does not reduce carboxylic acids .

Reactions of RCO 2 H-1
Esterification (see Section 16.8) In the presence of an acid catalyst, carboxylic acids and alcohols react to form esters (Fischer esterification). The reaction is an equilibrium process, but can be driven to completion by removing the water that is formed
